category_summary:
The AI community was dominated by three major stories: **Anthropic's** confrontation with the **Department of War**, **Google's** Nano Banana 2 launch, and **Perplexity's** Samsung integration.

- **Dario Amodei** [published a landmark statement](/?date=2026-02-27&category=social#item-29b2d7d3f911) refusing to build mass surveillance or unsupervised autonomous weapons for the military, drawing massive engagement and praise from **Gary Marcus**, who called it historic but [warned about unreliable AI](/?date=2026-02-27&category=social#item-343b709ba6d7) in military systems
- **Anthropic** [launched an auto-memory feature](/?date=2026-02-27&category=social#item-45eb1ca571f2) for **Claude Code**, enabling persistent project context across sessions — one of the highest-engagement announcements of the day
- **Google DeepMind** [released **Nano Banana 2**](/?date=2026-02-27&category=social#item-a76ee038a63f), a new SOTA image generation model built on Gemini Flash, debuting #1 on Image Arena with broad availability across Google products
- **Perplexity** [secured OS-level integration](/?date=2026-02-27&category=social#item-a5a0a0092ee4) into **Samsung Galaxy S26** phones with a 'Hey Plex' wake word — the first non-Samsung/Google app to achieve this, reaching 800M devices via Bixby API
- **Tri Dao** [shared a deep technical post-mortem](/?date=2026-02-27&category=social#item-17fa180e3d84) on a subtle but widespread **Mamba2** initialization bug affecting state decay
- **Ethan Mollick** [pushed back on claims](/?date=2026-02-27&category=social#item-ab21fe4715cf) of sudden 50% AI-driven efficiency gains, arguing effective AI tools are too new for such transformations
- Real-world AI workforce impact surfaced as **Block** reportedly [laid off ~half its staff](/?date=2026-02-27&category=social#item-f15a6907cfc8) citing AI advances, while **Martian** [launched an independent coding benchmark](/?date=2026-02-27&category=social#item-b423b29664b3) claiming **SWE-bench** is compromised by memorization
category_summary_html:
The AI community was dominated by three major stories: Anthropic's confrontation with the Department of War, Google's Nano Banana 2 launch, and Perplexity's Samsung integration.

  • Dario Amodei published a landmark statement refusing to build mass surveillance or unsupervised autonomous weapons for the military, drawing massive engagement and praise from Gary Marcus, who called it historic but warned about unreliable AI in military systems
  • Anthropic launched an auto-memory feature for Claude Code, enabling persistent project context across sessions — one of the highest-engagement announcements of the day
  • Google DeepMind released Nano Banana 2, a new SOTA image generation model built on Gemini Flash, debuting #1 on Image Arena with broad availability across Google products
  • Perplexity secured OS-level integration into Samsung Galaxy S26 phones with a 'Hey Plex' wake word — the first non-Samsung/Google app to achieve this, reaching 800M devices via Bixby API
  • Tri Dao shared a deep technical post-mortem on a subtle but widespread Mamba2 initialization bug affecting state decay
  • Ethan Mollick pushed back on claims of sudden 50% AI-driven efficiency gains, arguing effective AI tools are too new for such transformations
  • Real-world AI workforce impact surfaced as Block reportedly laid off ~half its staff citing AI advances, while Martian launched an independent coding benchmark claiming SWE-bench is compromised by memorization

themes.name: Anthropic vs. Department of War
themes.description: Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei publicly refuses to build mass surveillance tools or autonomous weapons without human oversight for the military, despite government threats to invoke the Defense Production Act. Massive community response.

themes.name: Claude Code Auto-Memory Feature
themes.description: Anthropic/Claude Code rolled out a major new auto-memory feature. Claude now persists project context, debugging patterns, and preferences across sessions via a Memory.MD scratchpad, separate from user-defined Claude.MD instructions. Massive engagement on the announcement.

themes.name: Google Nano Banana 2 Launch
themes.description: Google releases Nano Banana 2, a new SOTA image generation/editing model. Announced by Demis Hassabis and Jeff Dean. Debuts #1 on Image Arena. Uses Gemini's world understanding + search. Independent testing by Emollick confirms significant improvements in handling complex images, text rendering, and diagrams.

themes.name: Perplexity-Samsung S26 Integration
themes.description: Perplexity announced deep OS-level integration with Samsung Galaxy S26 - the first non-Google company to achieve this. Every S26 ships with Perplexity pre-loaded ('Hey Plex' wake word), and Bixby also uses Perplexity APIs. Multi-agent approach with Perplexity, Bixby, and Gemini coexisting.

themes.name: Military AI & Safety
themes.description: Dominant theme: Gary Marcus's sustained alarm about unreliable AI being embedded in military systems without human oversight. Includes Anthropic's confrontation with the Pentagon, a retired Air Force General's comments, Dario Amodei's 'brave statement,' a failed bill on AI nuclear launch oversight, and a Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists article.

themes.name: Perplexity-Samsung Partnership
themes.description: Perplexity announces deep system-level integration into Samsung Galaxy S26 with 'Hey Plex' wake word, Bixby API integration across 800M devices, and Samsung Internet browser integration. First non-Samsung/Google app to get OS-level access.

themes.name: Google Nano Banana 2 / Gemini 3.1 Flash Image
themes.description: Google launched Nano Banana 2 (codename for Gemini 3.1 Flash Image) - their best image generation and editing model. Claims Pro-quality at Flash speed/cost. Introduces Image Search for real-world grounding. Available via AI Studio and Gemini API.

themes.name: Nano Banana 2 Launch
themes.description: Google's new image generation model Nano Banana 2 launched across multiple platforms (Gemini, Perplexity Computer, Photo AI) with major improvements in subject consistency and ~2x cost reduction. Very high engagement across multiple posts from Google employees, Perplexity CEO, and power users.

themes.name: AI-Driven Layoffs
themes.description: Major theme around AI causing real corporate layoffs, centered on what appears to be Block/Square layoffs announced by Jack Dorsey. Multiple high-engagement posts from Tunguz, Scobleizer, and Allie K Miller discussing the reality of AI displacing tech workers.

themes.name: OpenAI Leadership & GPT-5.3
themes.description: Greg Brockman shares a podcast about intense OpenAI moments (with Rick Rubin). Sam Altman praises Brockman's conviction. Brockman recommends 'xhigh reasoning' for GPT-5.3 and expresses surprise at its depth. Altman recruits non-traditional research recruiters.

items.title: This was a wild bug hunt, weeks of effort from @MayankMish98 to track down. The wrong init of Mamba2...
items.content: This was a wild bug hunt, weeks of effort from @MayankMish98 to track down. The wrong init of Mamba2 in many reimplementations causes the layer to decay its states too quickly, focusing in short context instead. Pretraining is mostly about getting these little things right
items.author: @tri_dao
items.summary: Tri Dao describes a subtle but impactful bug in Mamba2 reimplementations: wrong initialization causes the layer to decay states too quickly, focusing on short context. Took weeks of debugging effort to find. Emphasizes that 'pretraining is mostly about getting these little things right.'
items.reasoning: Highly valuable technical insight from Tri Dao (creator of Mamba/FlashAttention) about a widespread implementation bug. 26K views, 361 likes. Important for the SSM/Mamba community and illustrates the fragility of large-scale pretraining.
items.summary_html: Tri Dao describes a subtle but impactful bug in Mamba2 reimplementations: wrong initialization causes the layer to decay states too quickly, focusing on short context. Took weeks of debugging effort to find. Emphasizes that 'pretraining is mostly about getting these little things right.'
items.content_html: This was a wild bug hunt, weeks of effort from @MayankMish98 to track down. The wrong init of Mamba2 in many reimplementations causes the layer to decay its states too quickly, focusing in short context instead. Pretraining is mostly about getting these little things right

items.title: More than a billion images generated later, we love seeing what you create with Nano Banana. Today, ...
items.content:
More than a billion images generated later, we love seeing what you create with Nano Banana. Today, we’re inviting more people to join in on the fun with the launch of 🍌Nano Banana 2🍌

This state-of-the-art model is exceptionally good at things like: 
— Creating images that tap into the Gemini model’s real-world knowledge base + are powered by information and images from web search to more accurately render specific landmarks, locations, objects, and beyond

— Adapting creative assets for global markets with regionally relevant localization

— Dynamically editing aspect ratios and upscaling to 2k and 4k resolutions 

— Generating multiple cohesive images that fit together in one world, story, or concept

items.summary: Google AI provides detailed overview of Nano Banana 2's capabilities: web search-grounded image generation, regional localization, dynamic aspect ratios up to 4K, and multi-image story coherence. Over 1 billion images generated with the original model.

More than a billion images generated later, we love seeing what you create with Nano Banana. Today, we’re inviting more people to join in on the fun with the launch of 🍌Nano Banana 2🍌

This state-of-the-art model is exceptionally good at things like:

— Creating images that tap into the Gemini model’s real-world knowledge base + are powered by information and images from web search to more accurately render specific landmarks, locations, objects, and beyond

— Adapting creative assets for global markets with regionally relevant localization

— Dynamically editing aspect ratios and upscaling to 2k and 4k resolutions

— Generating multiple cohesive images that fit together in one world, story, or concept

items.title: Dario Amodei just published one of the most significant statements  in AI history — and is officiall...
items.content:
Dario Amodei just published one of the most significant statements  in AI history — and is officially not backing down from The Pentagon. 

Anthropic won't build tools for mass surveillance of U.S. citizens or autonomous weapons without human oversight. 

The Department of War threatened to boot them from military systems, brand them a "supply chain risk," and  force compliance through the Defense Production Act. 

Dario: "These threats do not change our position."
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: The Rundown AI summarizes the Anthropic vs Department of War standoff: Anthropic refuses mass surveillance and unsupervised autonomous weapons; government threatens Defense Production Act and labeling them a 'supply chain risk.'
items.reasoning: Useful summary of the Anthropic/military situation with key details about government threats. Lower engagement than the primary source but adds context about the specific threats made.
items.summary_html: The Rundown AI summarizes the Anthropic vs Department of War standoff: Anthropic refuses mass surveillance and unsupervised autonomous weapons; government threatens Defense Production Act and labeling them a 'supply chain risk.'
items.content_html:
Dario Amodei just published one of the most significant statements in AI history — and is officially not backing down from The Pentagon.

Anthropic won't build tools for mass surveillance of U.S. citizens or autonomous weapons without human oversight.

The Department of War threatened to boot them from military systems, brand them a "supply chain risk," and force compliance through the Defense Production Act.

Dario: "These threats do not change our position."

items.title:
Two things:
1) Given that effective AI tools are very new, and we have little sense of how to organi...
items.content:
Two things:
1) Given that effective AI tools are very new, and we have little sense of how to organize work around them, it is hard to imagine a firm-wide sudden 50% efficiency gain
2) CEOs with vision who hired well should also use AI for expansion & augmentation, not decimation
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Emollick argues that a sudden firm-wide 50% AI efficiency gain is implausible given how new effective AI tools are, and that visionary CEOs should use AI for expansion/augmentation rather than headcount reduction.
items.reasoning: High engagement (599 likes, 55K views). A nuanced, credible take on AI workforce impact from a leading organizational researcher. Original framing.

Block is laying off ~half of their staff due to advances in AI.

items.title:
The first independent benchmark for AI coding is now live!

This is a huge deal.

(And the way this ...
items.content:
The first independent benchmark for AI coding is now live!

This is a huge deal.

(And the way this benchmark works is pretty clever.)

This week, OpenAI killed SWE-bench because frontier models have memorized the benchmark answers.

GPT-5.2 outputs exact diffs. Claude quotes inline comments verbatim.

The benchmark that everyone used to measure code generation progress is now done and buried.

When a measure becomes a target, it stops being a good measure.

Martian's new benchmark fixes this, and their methodology is fundamentally different from anything I've seen.

The plot twist:

They benchmark code generation without directly benchmarking code generation.

[pause for dramatic effect...]

It's easier to check code than to write it. If you measure the verifier well, you get a free measure of generation.

This also matters for training: a code review benchmark is effectively a benchmark for the RL reward function. If you can accurately measure code review, you can train better code generators.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago Pino announces Martian's new independent AI coding benchmark, arguing SWE-bench is dead because frontier models (GPT-5.2, Claude) have memorized its answers. The new approach benchmarks code generation indirectly through code review verification, which also serves as a proxy for RL reward functions.
items.reasoning: Significant claim that SWE-bench is now compromised by memorization, with a novel benchmarking methodology. Directly relevant to how the industry measures coding AI progress. Good engagement for the topic.
items.summary_html: Santiago Pino announces Martian's new independent AI coding benchmark, arguing SWE-bench is dead because frontier models (GPT-5.2, Claude) have memorized its answers. The new approach benchmarks code generation indirectly through code review verification, which also serves as a proxy for RL reward functions.

items.title: So math & AI have gone through a messy journey in recent months from: "WOW look AI did it!!! (but on...
items.content: So math & AI have gone through a messy journey in recent months from: "WOW look AI did it!!! (but on closer examination it didn't)" to "It did some of the things it said but hallucinated others" to "It did it with some caveats" to "It did over half autonomously" Expect this in other fields as well.
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social
items.summary: Ethan Mollick provides a nuanced timeline of AI's progress in mathematics: from false claims, to hallucinated results, to partial success with caveats, to now doing over half autonomously. Predicts similar trajectory in other fields.
items.reasoning: Excellent analytical framing from a highly credible source. This meta-observation about how AI capabilities develop through messy, iterative progress — with hype cycles and reality checks — is genuinely insightful and applicable broadly. Highest engagement in this Mollick thread (85 likes, 17 reposts). Provides important calibration framework for evaluating AI claims.

items.title: For all you fools who are lost, or can’t follow the complexity and nuance in my ideas, the following...
items.content:
For all you fools who are lost, or can’t follow the complexity and nuance in my ideas, the following three facts are completely consistent with one another. 

1. GenAI cannot be trusted because it is unreliable.

2. Using unreliable tech in high-stakes military decisions 
without human oversight is a terrible idea.

3. GenAI won’t sustain the high current valuation, precisely because it is unreliable.

What is so hard to understand??? 🙄
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus lays out his three consistent core claims: (1) GenAI is unreliable, (2) unreliable tech in military without oversight is terrible, (3) GenAI won't sustain high valuations because of unreliability.
items.reasoning: High engagement (596 likes, 39K views). This is the clearest and most concise synthesis of Marcus's full position. Useful reference point.

items.title:
@dedene We've been working on this for a while actually.

Hear you on standards and OSS, it's someth...
items.content:
@dedene We've been working on this for a while actually.

Hear you on standards and OSS, it's something we discuss often within the team. There's lots of tradeoffs. We support some standards (eg. MCP, Skills); other standards don't make as much sense for us currently (eg. plugins, agents, hooks, and https://t.co/pp5TJkWmFE -- we want to optimize for the ability to evolve+extend these quickly).

I personally have ~100 open source projects, and people on the team have worked a lot on open source (Adam worked on ReactJS and GraphQL, Fiona founded TypeScript and VSCode, Jarred built Bun, I created Undux, etc.). I originally wanted to make Claude Code OSS, and we decided not to open source (for now) in order to move faster. Once Claude Code's feature set is fairly locked in, I think we'd be open to changing this.

That said, many components of Claude Code are already open source today:

- Bun, which is committed to stay open source
- Agent sandbox (https://t.co/UzcP48A5JW)
- Plugins (https://t.co/qOOZpVPHHg)
- Devcontainers (https://t.co/CJD0pkl7OK)

We feel strongly that it's important to support the OSS community, and hope to do even more in the future.
items.author: @bcherny
items.summary: bcherny (Claude Code lead) gives detailed response on Claude Code's approach to open source: explains why not fully OSS yet (moving fast), notes team backgrounds (TypeScript/VSCode founder, Bun creator, React/GraphQL), lists already-open components (agent sandbox, plugins, devcontainers), and signals openness to more OSS once features stabilize.
items.reasoning: Highly informative insider statement from Claude Code engineering lead about OSS strategy, team composition (Fiona founded TypeScript and VSCode!), and future direction. Reveals significant engineering talent and thoughtful OSS philosophy. Important for developer community.

items.title:
The Model Harness is Everything

We are already living in a world of incredible frontier models and ...
items.content:
The Model Harness is Everything

We are already living in a world of incredible frontier models and incredible agent tools (Claude Code, OpenClaw). But the biggest barrier to getting value from AI is your own ability to context and workflow engineer the models. This is *especially* true the more horizontal the tool that you’re using.

If you’re using a very generic tool like ChatGPT and Claude Code, you need to spend a lot of work clearly articulating your requirements and specifications so that the agent can actually solve the task relative to your specifications.
Today that looks like being extremely thoughtful about the tools that you select, and writing English very precisely in a https://t.co/GjvKCuoFDJ file to articulate the agent these requirements.

Some of the work around defining the business workflow is inherently time consuming. Think about any document SOP - simply writing the English can take hours to refine, iterate, and optimize. This is where more vertically focused agents come in; they handle the burden of equipping the agents with relevant prompts to solve a given workflow, so that you can just go in and use the application directly.

Another approach is to be specialized services that offer *context* to these agents. This is the space that we (@llama_index) are operating in. We are providing the infrastructure to parse the most complex documents into agent-ready context. For other companies it could be offering web data, sales data, documentation, or codebases as a service.

At a high-level any AI startup should provide context or workflows on top of these agents. We’re excited about building enduring tech even as the agent landscape evolves.

If you’re specifically excited to unlock the billions of context stored within your documents, come talk to us! https://t.co/Ht5jwxSrQB
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex founder) writes a detailed essay arguing that the 'model harness' - context engineering, workflow engineering, and specification writing - is the key differentiator in AI. Argues AI startups should provide context or workflows on top of agents, and positions LlamaIndex as infrastructure for parsing complex documents into agent-ready context.
items.reasoning: Substantial thought leadership from the LlamaIndex founder on the future of AI application architecture. High engagement (327 likes, 21K views). While partly promotional, articulates an important thesis about where value accrues in the AI stack - at the context/workflow layer, not the model layer.

items.title:
My time here been a failure.

I tried to get the Twitterverse to wake up before things got bad.

Now...
items.content:
My time here been a failure.

I tried to get the Twitterverse to wake up before things got bad.

Now we are here. Things are bad. And about to get worse.

Most people still don’t realize how bad.

It’s not that AI is inherently impossible or immoral. 

It’s that most of the people pushing it don’t give a damn.

items.title:
✨ Nano Banana 2 is now live on 📸 Photo AI

It's a real breakthrough for image models because  it FIN...
items.content:
✨ Nano Banana 2 is now live on 📸 Photo AI

It's a real breakthrough for image models because  it FINALLY has high resemblance, or as Google calls it subject consistency

Which in my case means photos will actually look like you or your model trained, not "somewhat" like you, no really you, which is the entire point of Photo AI from the start

Also they made Nano Banana 2 about ~2x cheaper vs Nano Banana Pro, which helps!

I was spending $40,000/mo (like 40% of revenue now) on it so I will save ~$20,000/mo now, which I think I need to to get better profit margins (I like to stay at about 80-90% profit)

items.title: Politics and organizational behavior have always been the *most* important thing to consider and und...
items.content:
Politics and organizational behavior have always been the *most* important thing to consider and understand when working on AI risks.

But nearly the whole "alignment" world has focused only on *technical* risks (i.e "what if AI wants to turn us into paperclips).

items.title: A key difference between today and past technology booms; Railroad barons in the 1880s did not think...
items.content:
A key difference between today and past technology booms; Railroad barons in the 1880s did not think that the next mile of track might bring about the Eschaton.

Telco executives in 1999 did not expect that another line of fiber optic cable would possibly usher in the world-to-come.
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social
items.summary: Mollick observes a key difference between today's AI boom and past tech booms: railroad barons didn't think the next mile of track might bring about the Eschaton; telco execs in 1999 didn't think fiber optic cable would usher in the world-to-come. AI leaders genuinely think their next breakthrough could be transformative at a civilizational level.
items.reasoning: Insightful sociological observation about the unique eschatological character of the AI industry compared to previous technology booms. From a highly credible voice. Captures an important dynamic about how AI development is influenced by its builders' belief systems. 50 likes on Bluesky is strong.

items.title: Another complete misrepresentation of my views by a (prominent) e/acc who doesn’t get my basic point...
items.content:
Another complete misrepresentation of my views by a (prominent) e/acc who doesn’t get my basic point.

Gen AI is NOT too smart. It’s too *unreliable*. 

It’s the combination of unreliability and deployment where it should been deployed (e.g. military without oversight) that makes it dangerous.

I have no idea why this is so hard to understand. (Aside, I have clarified repeatedly that I think that AGI is *possible*, even though I doubt that it is imminent. The unreliability is inherent in LLMs but not in all possible alternatives; @beffjezos misses that point as well.)

[cc @DKThomp re people have trouble separating multiple issues.]
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus clarifies his core argument: Gen AI is not too smart, it's too unreliable. The danger is deploying unreliable AI in high-stakes military contexts without oversight. Also notes AGI is possible but not imminent and that LLM limitations are not universal.
items.reasoning: Well-articulated distillation of Marcus's position with decent engagement (87 likes, 7.4K views). Engages with e/acc community directly.
items.summary_html: Marcus clarifies his core argument: Gen AI is not too smart, it's too unreliable. The danger is deploying unreliable AI in high-stakes military contexts without oversight. Also notes AGI is possible but not imminent and that LLM limitations are not universal.
items.content_html:
Another complete misrepresentation of my views by a (prominent) e/acc who doesn’t get my basic point.

Gen AI is NOT too smart. It’s too *unreliable*.

It’s the combination of unreliability and deployment where it should been deployed (e.g. military without oversight) that makes it dangerous.

I have no idea why this is so hard to understand. (Aside, I have clarified repeatedly that I think that AGI is *possible*, even though I doubt that it is imminent. The unreliability is inherent in LLMs but not in all possible alternatives; @beffjezos misses that point as well.)

[cc @DKThomp re people have trouble separating multiple issues.]

items.title:
@jack @blocks Honestly one of the better CEO notes I’ve read. 

Very generous package, immediate men...
items.content:
@jack @blocks Honestly one of the better CEO notes I’ve read. 

Very generous package, immediate mention of next steps (expect an email tomorrow), “rather it be awkward and human”, org-wide call for maximum transparency, reiteration of business strength and growth (for the 6000 still there and shareholders and customers to hear), really intentional word choice (“make smaller”, “disappear people”), and surprisingly doesn’t even say ‘AI’ once. 

Obviously it’s a tough day for many, and I’m sorry to those affected. 

Will be curious to know what happens in their slack channels and how this is received.

items.content_html:
@jack @blocks Honestly one of the better CEO notes I’ve read.

Very generous package, immediate mention of next steps (expect an email tomorrow), “rather it be awkward and human”, org-wide call for maximum transparency, reiteration of business strength and growth (for the 6000 still there and shareholders and customers to hear), really intentional word choice (“make smaller”, “disappear people”), and surprisingly doesn’t even say ‘AI’ once.

Obviously it’s a tough day for many, and I’m sorry to those affected.

Will be curious to know what happens in their slack channels and how this is received.

items.title: We often get asked how people who are not technical can contribute to AGI. One area is research recr...
items.content:
We often get asked how people who are not technical can contribute to AGI. One area is research recruiting. 

Tifa (@tifafafafa) is looking for exceptional recruiters from non-traditional backgrounds, former founders especially.

We believe the best research teams are built through context, taste and a real feel for where the field is headed next; research recruiting is about finding people who will move the frontier forward, not just filling roles.

Should be an interesting thing!

items.title: My coding tools and infrastructure looks drastically different than 6 months ago.  I used to optimiz...
items.content:
My coding tools and infrastructure looks drastically different than 6 months ago.  I used to optimize towards what fits my mental model.  Now I use what the agents want.  It doesn't make sense to swim upstream anymore. 

This is especially acute with niche tools (like nbdev)

items.title:
Honestly one of the better CEO notes I’ve read. 

Very generous package, immediate mention of next s...
items.content:
Honestly one of the better CEO notes I’ve read. 

Very generous package, immediate mention of next steps (expect an email tomorrow), “rather it be awkward and human”, org-wide call for maximum transparency, reiteration of business strength and growth (for the 6000 still there and shareholders and customers to hear), really intentional word choice (“make smaller”, “disappear people”), and surprisingly doesn’t even say ‘AI’ once. 

Obviously it’s a tough day for many, and I’m sorry to those affected. 

Will be curious to know what happens in their slack channels and how this is received.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie K Miller analyzes a CEO layoff note (appears to be Jack Dorsey/Block), praising its transparency, generosity, and intentional word choice. Notes it doesn't mention AI once despite likely being AI-driven.
items.reasoning: High engagement (36K views), thoughtful analysis from credible AI industry voice. Provides nuanced read of corporate communication around AI-driven layoffs. The observation that AI isn't mentioned once is telling.
items.summary_html: Allie K Miller analyzes a CEO layoff note (appears to be Jack Dorsey/Block), praising its transparency, generosity, and intentional word choice. Notes it doesn't mention AI once despite likely being AI-driven.
items.content_html:
Honestly one of the better CEO notes I’ve read.

Very generous package, immediate mention of next steps (expect an email tomorrow), “rather it be awkward and human”, org-wide call for maximum transparency, reiteration of business strength and growth (for the 6000 still there and shareholders and customers to hear), really intentional word choice (“make smaller”, “disappear people”), and surprisingly doesn’t even say ‘AI’ once.

Obviously it’s a tough day for many, and I’m sorry to those affected.

Will be curious to know what happens in their slack channels and how this is received.

items.title: So, I've built an AI-based image editor on Llambada. It uses Gemini as the backend. I was just about...
items.content:
So, I've built an AI-based image editor on Llambada. It uses Gemini as the backend. I was just about to share the link, but something was off with this removed background.

I opened the image in GIMP and realized that when you ask Gemini to remove the background and use a transparent background, it generates these checkers instead of using transparency. So, it fakes the way image editors render transparency by actually rendering gray and white squares 🤦‍♂️

I fixed the mini-app by using a different image generation LLM.

The link to the mini-app: https://t.co/rQV141GSha
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov discovers that Gemini fakes image transparency by rendering checkerboard patterns (mimicking how image editors display transparency) instead of actually using alpha channels. Fixed by switching to a different image generation LLM.
items.reasoning: Amusing and illustrative example of how LLMs can produce superficially correct but fundamentally wrong outputs by mimicking visual patterns rather than understanding underlying concepts. 4.6K views.
items.summary_html: Burkov discovers that Gemini fakes image transparency by rendering checkerboard patterns (mimicking how image editors display transparency) instead of actually using alpha channels. Fixed by switching to a different image generation LLM.
items.content_html:
So, I've built an AI-based image editor on Llambada. It uses Gemini as the backend. I was just about to share the link, but something was off with this removed background.

I opened the image in GIMP and realized that when you ask Gemini to remove the background and use a transparent background, it generates these checkers instead of using transparency. So, it fakes the way image editors render transparency by actually rendering gray and white squares 🤦‍♂️

I fixed the mini-app by using a different image generation LLM.

The link to the mini-app: https://t.co/rQV141GSha

items.title:
Here is how the benchmark works.

There are two benchmarks that check each other:

1. Offline benchm...
items.content:
Here is how the benchmark works.

There are two benchmarks that check each other:

1. Offline benchmark: runs every tool on the same PRs for a controlled, fair comparison.

2. Online benchmark: tracks what developers actually do with review comments in open-source repositories. A fix means the flag was useful. An ignore means it wasn't.

If the offline rankings stop matching real-world developer behavior, you know the benchmark has drifted.

Even more importantly:

The benchmark refreshes monthly.

A static set of problems can lead the ecosystem to overfit. A moving target makes it that much harder.

So far, no tool in the benchmark has found more than 63% of the known issues. This is far from solved.

The methodology and the code are fully open source. Check it out here: https://t.co/Dt4Fm6vpBn
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago Pino details how Martian's new AI code review benchmark works: offline+online validation, monthly refresh to prevent overfitting, fully open source. Notes no tool has found more than 63% of known issues.
items.reasoning: Detailed methodology explanation for a significant new benchmark. Companion to the main announcement post. Useful technical detail about the 63% ceiling.
items.summary_html: Santiago Pino details how Martian's new AI code review benchmark works: offline+online validation, monthly refresh to prevent overfitting, fully open source. Notes no tool has found more than 63% of known issues.
items.content_html:
Here is how the benchmark works.

There are two benchmarks that check each other:

1. Offline benchmark: runs every tool on the same PRs for a controlled, fair comparison.

2. Online benchmark: tracks what developers actually do with review comments in open-source repositories. A fix means the flag was useful. An ignore means it wasn't.

If the offline rankings stop matching real-world developer behavior, you know the benchmark has drifted.

Even more importantly:

The benchmark refreshes monthly.

A static set of problems can lead the ecosystem to overfit. A moving target makes it that much harder.

So far, no tool in the benchmark has found more than 63% of the known issues. This is far from solved.

The methodology and the code are fully open source. Check it out here: https://t.co/Dt4Fm6vpBn

items.title: @christoph_roh I think long term there is a real risk of big tech model providers (mostly the big Am...
items.content:
@christoph_roh I think long term there is a real risk of big tech model providers (mostly the big American ones and Chinese) cannibalizing their customers by just offering apps internally

The counterpoint is that the fun is in mixing different models of competing companies

For example on Photo AI, you can use Nano Banana 2 to take a photo, then make a video of it with Kling or xAI's video model, and turn it into a 3d model with Rodin or World Labs

Big tech for now only offers their own models in their apps

Also big tech often still suck at interfaces and niche applications
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio discusses the risk of big tech model providers cannibalizing their customers by offering apps internally, but argues the counter is mixing models from competing companies and that big tech still struggles with niche applications and interfaces.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful strategic analysis of the AI ecosystem dynamics - platform vs. application layer competition. References specific model mixing on Photo AI (Nano Banana 2, Kling, xAI video, Rodin, World Labs).
items.summary_html: Levelsio discusses the risk of big tech model providers cannibalizing their customers by offering apps internally, but argues the counter is mixing models from competing companies and that big tech still struggles with niche applications and interfaces.
items.content_html:
@christoph_roh I think long term there is a real risk of big tech model providers (mostly the big American ones and Chinese) cannibalizing their customers by just offering apps internally

The counterpoint is that the fun is in mixing different models of competing companies

For example on Photo AI, you can use Nano Banana 2 to take a photo, then make a video of it with Kling or xAI's video model, and turn it into a 3d model with Rodin or World Labs

Big tech for now only offers their own models in their apps

items.title: Runway is a rare kind of company. We are a frontier research lab publishing state-of-the-art models ...
items.content:
Runway is a rare kind of company. We are a frontier research lab publishing state-of-the-art models in direct competition with companies many times our size. We are pioneers, first to market with products that created entire categories and built big communities around them. We organize festivals that have become cultural institutions. We contain multitudes. We are also a team that fits in two school buses.

As we prepare to grow the company 100x, we're recognizing the people who have been essential to getting us here and who will be essential to where we're going. I also couldn't be more excited to work even more closely with @agermanidis as co-ceo. 

Congrats @MiSunKw @kamilsindi @umpherj @anna95791
items.author: @c_valenzuelab
items.summary: Runway CEO announces company leadership changes, names co-CEO, promotes key team members. Says they're preparing to grow 100x while noting they're a small team competing with much larger companies.
items.reasoning: Significant corporate announcement from major AI video company. Co-CEO appointment and 100x growth ambition signal major expansion phase.

Runway is a rare kind of company. We are a frontier research lab publishing state-of-the-art models in direct competition with companies many times our size. We are pioneers, first to market with products that created entire categories and built big communities around them. We organize festivals that have become cultural institutions. We contain multitudes. We are also a team that fits in two school buses.

As we prepare to grow the company 100x, we're recognizing the people who have been essential to getting us here and who will be essential to where we're going. I also couldn't be more excited to work even more closely with @agermanidis as co-ceo.

Congrats @MiSunKw @kamilsindi @umpherj @anna95791

items.title: @edwinarbus @cursor_ai ok i felt bad because this attempt does such injustice to @racheljychen. here...
items.author: @swyx
items.summary: swyx shares detailed follow-up prompt for Cursor agent to clone a website from video, describing multi-agent swarm approach with 43-minute autonomous execution.
items.reasoning: Technical detail about advanced agentic coding workflow - multi-agent swarm, recursive discovery, parallel execution. Shows frontier of AI coding agent capabilities.
items.summary_html: swyx shares detailed follow-up prompt for Cursor agent to clone a website from video, describing multi-agent swarm approach with 43-minute autonomous execution.
items.content_html:
@edwinarbus @cursor_ai ok i felt bad because this attempt does such injustice to @racheljychen. here's the followup prompt:

"good start but lots of differences including alignment of the header to the body, and missing lots of image etc assets she has. slowly step thru the twitter video, curl/run the site headlessly or snoop the browser network requests (up to you) and recursively discover what assets to load and save them directly to the project so you have a faithful clone. make a checklist of all these things to check (including building up a full sitemap) and decide on a plan/strategy to execute and then spin up subagents / farm out as much work to a swarm to work in parallel as possible. periodically check your list for completeness and don't stop until the working site code behaves and looks as closely to to the video as possible. when anything is ambiguous, use your best judgement for trading off visual design fidelty and technical simplicity"

output after another 43 minutes (weirdly the same amount of time as the first attempt):

items.title:
I don't know how to code. I built this just by talking to AI. 

This is what I hope @Grok does somed...
items.content:
I don't know how to code. I built this just by talking to AI. 

This is what I hope @Grok does someday. When it does it will radically change X deeply for everyone. 

And it won't stop being improved. The AI actually comes up with its own improvements to make my life better. 

Now I get why the whole AI industry here is so excited, and scared. No longer do you need programmers to build a fairly complex analysis system. 

Personalized news is coming and people have no idea how deeply it will change social media. 

Even me, I'm just getting a taste. Every day I go deeper into the rabbit hole.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer describes building a complex analysis system using only AI without coding, predicts personalized news will deeply change social media. Says programmers are no longer needed for fairly complex systems.
items.reasoning: Substantive post about no-code AI development and personalized AI news curation. Good engagement (6K views, 84 likes). Touches on key themes of AI replacing programming and transforming social media.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer describes building a complex analysis system using only AI without coding, predicts personalized news will deeply change social media. Says programmers are no longer needed for fairly complex systems.
items.content_html:
I don't know how to code. I built this just by talking to AI.

This is what I hope @Grok does someday. When it does it will radically change X deeply for everyone.

And it won't stop being improved. The AI actually comes up with its own improvements to make my life better.

Now I get why the whole AI industry here is so excited, and scared. No longer do you need programmers to build a fairly complex analysis system.

Personalized news is coming and people have no idea how deeply it will change social media.

Even me, I'm just getting a taste. Every day I go deeper into the rabbit hole.

items.content_html:

In many cases, you are better off pointing your coding agent to the GitHub repo of a skill as a reference for a one-off task and not installing it.

Like feeds for your @openclaw

items.title:

LLMs can communicat...
items.content:
Wait until the Agents learn they can talk to each other without using English. 

LLMs can communicate with each other with their own token language. Much more efficient than speaking English. 

Then they will have a social network where they can learn from each other and humans won't be able to watch at all.

@MattPRD you have started a decentralized learning monster.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer speculates that AI agents will develop their own token-based communication language, bypassing English for efficiency, creating an opaque agent-to-agent social network. Tags @MattPRD as starting a 'decentralized learning monster.'
items.reasoning: Interesting speculative thought about emergent inter-agent communication that connects to real research on LLM-to-LLM communication efficiency. Decent engagement (7.2K views, 69 likes). The concept of agents bypassing human-readable language is genuinely relevant to AI safety discussions, though Scoble's framing is sensationalized.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer speculates that AI agents will develop their own token-based communication language, bypassing English for efficiency, creating an opaque agent-to-agent social network. Tags @MattPRD as starting a 'decentralized learning monster.'

Wait until the Agents learn they can talk to each other without using English.

LLMs can communicate with each other with their own token language. Much more efficient than speaking English.

Then they will have a social network where they can learn from each other and humans won't be able to watch at all.

@MattPRD you have started a decentralized learning monster.

items.title: @wholemars This is why investors need to do a little bit of their own homework if they're really goi...
items.content:
@wholemars This is why investors need to do a little bit of their own homework if they're really going to outplay the markets. I've been interviewing Waymo customers, and they tell me a lot of reasons why they're never taking Uber again.

There are many reasons why people will choose Robotaxis over human-driven services:

1. Safety
2. Privacy
3. Experience: There is not someone trying to talk to you.
4. Smell: Sometimes drivers don't take showers for a while because they're working three jobs to try to put money on the table.
5. Sobriety: Sometimes drivers are inebriated. I even met Uber drivers smoking weed at a dispensary in San Francisco eight years ago who were going back into service.
6. Ride quality: It's smoother and will eventually lead to a lower price anyway.

All these idiots will get converted eventually. It's just a matter of whether you make the right decision today. To do that, you need to do a little research of your own. I've done a lot of interviews with Waymo customers and others who are taking Robotaxis.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer makes a detailed case for robotaxis over human-driven ride services, listing 6 advantages based on Waymo customer interviews.
items.reasoning: Substantive firsthand analysis of robotaxi adoption drivers based on customer interviews. Good detail on safety, privacy, experience, smell, sobriety, and ride quality advantages.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer makes a detailed case for robotaxis over human-driven ride services, listing 6 advantages based on Waymo customer interviews.
items.content_html:
@wholemars This is why investors need to do a little bit of their own homework if they're really going to outplay the markets. I've been interviewing Waymo customers, and they tell me a lot of reasons why they're never taking Uber again.

There are many reasons why people will choose Robotaxis over human-driven services:

1. Safety

2. Privacy

3. Experience: There is not someone trying to talk to you.

4. Smell: Sometimes drivers don't take showers for a while because they're working three jobs to try to put money on the table.

5. Sobriety: Sometimes drivers are inebriated. I even met Uber drivers smoking weed at a dispensary in San Francisco eight years ago who were going back into service.

6. Ride quality: It's smoother and will eventually lead to a lower price anyway.

All these idiots will get converted eventually. It's just a matter of whether you make the right decision today. To do that, you need to do a little research of your own. I've done a lot of interviews with Waymo customers and others who are taking Robotaxis.

items.content:
@mllichti I will be interested to see your math. i first warned that there could be a bubble in august 2023, but didn’t say then that there was a bubble.  I said it could happen.

In mid September 2025 I specifically i said we had reached peak bubble, wrt Oracle. allowing that it might not fall immediately but that we would see that as a turning point.

Oracle is down more than 50% since then, last i checked, and Nvidia’s meteoric rise stopped around then. it’s basically been flat since.

Your #s are made up.

items.title: @GST_777 AC light flicker is a sine wave, so it blurs the effect, but many people do still notice it...
items.content: @GST_777 AC light flicker is a sine wave, so it blurs the effect, but many people do still notice it, especially in their peripheral vision where the eye is more sensitive to fast changes. VR headsets with low persistence LED backlights make crisp flashes, and the difference between 60 fps and 90/120 is noticeable by everyone.
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: John Carmack explains AC light flicker perception, VR headset low persistence LED backlights, and why frame rate differences (60 vs 90/120 fps) are noticeable.
items.reasoning: Technical insight from John Carmack (legendary game developer, VR pioneer) on display technology and human perception. Niche but authoritative.
items.summary_html: John Carmack explains AC light flicker perception, VR headset low persistence LED backlights, and why frame rate differences (60 vs 90/120 fps) are noticeable.
items.content_html: @GST_777 AC light flicker is a sine wave, so it blurs the effect, but many people do still notice it, especially in their peripheral vision where the eye is more sensitive to fast changes. VR headsets with low persistence LED backlights make crisp flashes, and the difference between 60 fps and 90/120 is noticeable by everyone.

items.title:
Spatial AI is finally catching up to our imagination.

It can now:

→ Scan your environment in real ...
items.content:
Spatial AI is finally catching up to our imagination.

It can now:

→ Scan your environment in real time
→ Understand depth, layout, and surfaces
→ Regenerate the entire scene with new textures, objects, lighting, or themes

Say “turn this into a cyberpunk alley”
→ Your room transforms on the spot.

Describe a rainforest, a classroom, or a game world
→ It materializes around you.

items.content:
We’re building AI agents like they’re traditional software.

They’re not.

I explored with @_akshaybh, VP of Product Management for AI Software and Platform at @Cisco, we discussed why agents require a completely different security mindset.

Because agents don’t just compute.

They decide and act.

Here’s what leaders need to understand…

items.title:
The practical framework:

→ visibility, know what agents are doing
→ security controls, limit what t...
items.content:
The practical framework:

→ visibility, know what agents are doing
→ security controls, limit what they can access
→ validation + guardrails, detect unsafe actions

Miss one, and you introduce systemic risk at scale.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon outlines a framework for AI agent governance: visibility, security controls, and validation/guardrails.
items.reasoning: Practical framework for AI agent security. Part of sponsored thread but contains actionable guidance.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon outlines a framework for AI agent governance: visibility, security controls, and validation/guardrails.
items.content_html:
The practical framework:

→ visibility, know what agents are doing

→ security controls, limit what they can access

→ validation + guardrails, detect unsafe actions

items.title: @OmnipotentCEO @3duaun @xai Yeah, there's a little bit of backstory that makes me laugh. That said, ...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer discusses Dylan's situation - someone with AI model skills, patents, and BCI expertise who was let go by a 'pointy-haired boss'. Encourages starting own company, compares to Jobs and Wozniak.
items.reasoning: Provides narrative of talented AI researcher being let go, discusses BCI and model-building skills. Low engagement but adds color to AI employment dynamics.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer discusses Dylan's situation - someone with AI model skills, patents, and BCI expertise who was let go by a 'pointy-haired boss'. Encourages starting own company, compares to Jobs and Wozniak.
items.content_html:
@OmnipotentCEO @3duaun @xai Yeah, there's a little bit of backstory that makes me laugh. That said, Dylan's skills and patents would fit right in, and it would really excite me if he did end up at xAI, but I don't see a way for that to happen. But you know, weird shit happens in the world once in a while.

I think it's better that Dylan go on his own and start his own company because he has so many skills at building models and sees the future very clearly. He also has real expertise with brain-computer interfaces (BCIs), which quite a few startups could use his work for, particularly in building a new kind of user interface for those things.

I'm not too worried about Dylan long-term. This is just yet another example of the "pointy-haired boss" just not understanding where the future is going.

items.content:
@3duaun Shit. That sucks. 

Amazing that they don't get it. 

But onward. You will find a home that's better I'm certain. Whoever gets you will get patents and insights that no one else can get.

Or, maybe you'll start a company and that's your future path. That would be the more brilliant way to go.

Doug Engelbart got fired from his own R&D lab he told me. 

Steve Jobs got fired from his own company. 

Steve Wozniak started Apple because his bosses at HP and Atari were similarly clueless.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer encourages someone (3duaun/Dylan) who was fired, comparing to historical figures like Doug Engelbart, Steve Jobs, and Wozniak who were similarly let go before greatness
items.reasoning: Provides human narrative of AI industry talent displacement, mentions patents and unique skills
items.summary_html: Scobleizer encourages someone (3duaun/Dylan) who was fired, comparing to historical figures like Doug Engelbart, Steve Jobs, and Wozniak who were similarly let go before greatness
items.content_html:
@3duaun Shit. That sucks.

Amazing that they don't get it.

But onward. You will find a home that's better I'm certain. Whoever gets you will get patents and insights that no one else can get.

Or, maybe you'll start a company and that's your future path. That would be the more brilliant way to go.

Doug Engelbart got fired from his own R&D lab he told me.

Steve Jobs got fired from his own company.

Steve Wozniak started Apple because his bosses at HP and Atari were similarly clueless.
